unitTitle: Unit 4: Present perfect simple, present perfect continuous

content.subtitle: Use
items: Situations that started in the past and are still true
Completed actions at a time in the past which is not mentioned
Completed actions where the important thing is the result now

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Mrs Jenkins **has been** the head teacher for three years.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
notes: We don't use the present perfect simple when we want to say **when** something happened in the past. We use the past simple: ✓ I **did** my homework **last night**.
We don't use the past simple when we want to show that something happened before now or is still important now. We use the present perfect simple: ✓ I've **finished**! Can I go home now?
Some verbs have irregular past participle forms.

sections.title: A: Present perfect simple
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct present perfect simple form of the verbs in brackets.

items.sentence: I ...... (see) this film already.
John and Julie ...... (have) their car for about a year.
She ...... (not / take) her driving test yet.
Sue ...... (be) a tour guide since she left university.
...... (you / ride) into town on your new bike yet?
This new computer ...... (make) my life a lot easier.
We ...... (not / decide) what to get Mark for his birthday yet.
...... (Paul / ever / meet) a famous person?
...... never played this game before.
Adam ...... his room last night.
...... here since 2005?
Carol and I ...... to the cinema three nights ago.
It's the first time ...... our flat, isn't it?
They ...... the baby a name yet.
...... to New York when you went to the States last summer?
...... an e-mail before?

sections.title: C: Write sentences
sections.instructions: Look at the picture and use the prompts to write sentences. Use the correct form of the present perfect simple.

items.sentence: lesson / not / start / yet
teacher / already / write / on the board
items.answer: The teacher has already written on the board.

items.sentence: Joe and Tim / just / come / into the classroom
items.answer: Joe and Tim have just come into the classroom.

items.sentence: Tony / not / finished / getting / books ready
items.answer: Tony hasn't finished getting his books ready.

items.sentence: Christine / already / open / book
items.answer: Christine has already opened her book.

items.sentence: Dave / drop / pen / on the floor
items.answer: Dave has dropped his pen on the floor.

items.sentence: he / not / pick it up / yet
items.answer: He hasn't picked it up yet.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions continuing up to now or just before now

content.subtitle: Examples
items: We've **been doing** grammar exercises for over an hour. Can we have a break now?
They're having a break now because they've **been working** so hard.

content.subtitle: Helpful hints
content.description: The present perfect continuous is often used with the following words:
rows: I've been learning English **for** over three years.
I've **just** been reading the school newspaper.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
notes: The present perfect simple often emphasises the result of an action: ✓ She's **written** an article for the school newspaper. (= She's finished it.)

The present perfect continuous often emphasises the action, and the time spent on the action, rather than the result: ✓ She's **been writing** an article for the school newspaper. (= She's started, but she hasn't finished it yet.)

sections.title: D: Present perfect continuous
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct present perfect continuous form of the verbs in brackets. Use short forms where possible.
sections.passage:
Mandy: Hi Matt. How are you? What (1) ...... (you / do) recently?
Matt: Oh, hi Mandy! Well, (2) ...... (I / study) for my exams.
Mandy: That sounds boring! (3) ...... (you / work) hard?
Matt: Very! Basically, (4) ...... (I / just / sit) at my desk in my bedroom for the past three weeks and (5) ...... (I / not / go) out at all. (6) ...... (I / work) with Michael, my best friend, some of the time, though, so at least I've had some company. How about you?
Mandy: Well, my mum and (7) ...... (I / paint) my bedroom for the last few days. That has been fun! And (8) ...... (we / also / plan) our summer holiday.
Matt: Great! Where are you going?
Mandy: Well, we haven't decided yet. (9) ...... (We / look) at different places to see which we like best.
Matt: I'm sure you'll have a great time, wherever you go. Oh, by the way, (10) ...... (I / think) of having a party when I finish my exams. Would you like to come?
Mandy: Sure! That would be great!

sections.title: E: Circle the correct word or phrase
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: I think I've **heard / been hearing** that song before.
They haven't **arrived / been arriving** yet, but they should be here soon.
You've **written / been writing** that e-mail for over an hour. How long is it going to take you?
Have you **talked / been talking** on the phone since eight o'clock?
Jo has already **invited / been inviting** Shirley to dinner.
I've **read / been reading** an interview with Brad Pitt, but I haven't finished it yet.
Have the boys **played / been playing** computer games since this morning?

sections.title: F: Complete using the words in the box
sections.instructions: Complete using the words in the box.

items.sentence: I haven't listened to their new CD ...... . Is it any good?
We've been waiting for you ...... over an hour. Where have you been?
Have you ...... been to the UK before?
I'm afraid we've ...... made plans for this weekend, so we won't be free.
Pedro has been having English lessons ...... he was five years old.
It's strange that you mention the film Crash. I've ...... been reading about it in the paper.
I've ...... heard of a 'sudoku'. What is it?
